project.name: llm-gateway-example
project.description: Minimal runnable LLM gateway (Bun + Elysia + Vercel AI SDK): a thin layer in front of OpenAI/Google that centralizes secrets, per-app auth, rate-limiting, monthly token quota, Gemini→GPT fallback, cost tracking and observability, so calling apps don't reimplement these cross-cutting concerns.

nodes.name: README.md
nodes.summary: Overview of the minimal LLM gateway example; maps each cross-cutting concern (centralized secret, auth registry, rate-limit, quota, multi-provider fallback, cost tracking, observability, dashboard-lite) to where it lives in the code, plus run/test/curl instructions and a list of intentionally-omitted prod concerns.

nodes.name: package.json
nodes.summary: Bun + Elysia project manifest. Dependencies: Vercel AI SDK (@ai-sdk/openai, @ai-sdk/google, ai) and elysia. Scripts: dev/start run src/index.ts under Bun, test runs bun test.

nodes.name: .env.example
nodes.summary: Env template: provider API keys (OPENAI_API_KEY, GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY) and PORT. Kept server-side so client apps authenticate with a gateway key and never see provider keys.

nodes.name: index.ts
nodes.summary: Thin Elysia facade. Derives the app from the x-api-key header (401 on unknown key), exposes POST /v1/chat (delegates to handle, maps GateError to status) and GET /metrics, then listens on PORT (default 4000).
nodes.languageNotes: Uses Elysia .derive() to attach `app` to the request context after key validation; t.Object body schema validates the chat payload.

nodes.name: gateway.ts
nodes.summary: Gateway core: per-app registry + centralized secret, fixed-window rate limit, monthly token quota, ordered provider chain (Gemini→GPT) with 20s per-call timeout and fallback, per-app cost/usage metrics, and JSON-line observability. In-memory state (ponytail: swap to Redis+Postgres for prod).
nodes.languageNotes: Module-level singletons (APPS, windows, usage, CHAIN) hold in-memory state; handle() is the single gate function every request flows through.

nodes.name: ratelimit.ts
nodes.summary: Pure fixed-window rate limiter (requests/min): takes a window, rpm and clock value, returns {allowed, win}. Dependency-free so it can be unit-tested in isolation with an injected clock.
nodes.languageNotes: Now-injected (number) keeps the function deterministic and testable without timers.

nodes.name: gateway.test.ts
nodes.summary: Bun tests for the rate-limit money-path: allows up to rpm requests then blocks the next, and reopens after the window resets. Covers the core policy primitive rather than the full HTTP path.

nodes.name: POST /v1/chat
nodes.summary: Primary chat endpoint. Validates body {messages:[{role,content}]}, calls handle(app, messages), and maps a thrown GateError to its HTTP status (429/402/502).

nodes.name: GET /metrics
nodes.summary: Dashboard-lite endpoint returning per-app usage counters (requests, tokens, costUsd, fallbacks, errors) for the whole company.

nodes.name: appByKey
nodes.summary: Look up an AppConfig by its gateway API key; returns undefined for unknown keys. Used by index.ts auth derive.

nodes.name: handle
nodes.summary: Main gate. Enforces rate-limit (throws 429) and monthly token quota (throws 402), then runs the provider chain with a 20s per-provider timeout, falls back on error, records usage/cost, and returns {provider, text, tokens}. Throws 502 if all providers fail.

nodes.name: metrics
nodes.summary: Return a snapshot of the per-app usage map (requests, tokens, costUsd, fallbacks, errors).

nodes.name: estimateCost
nodes.summary: Estimate USD cost from a token count using a per-model price table (USD per 1M tokens).

nodes.name: bump
nodes.summary: Merge a partial usage patch into the per-app usage map, creating the entry on first write.

nodes.name: withTimeout
nodes.summary: Race a promise against a timeout rejection; used to cap each provider call so a slow provider triggers fallback.

nodes.name: log
nodes.summary: Emit one JSON line per request (timestamp, app, provider, tokens, ms, ok/fallback/error) to stdout. ponytail: prod pushes to OTel/log store.

nodes.name: rateLimit
nodes.summary: Pure fixed-window check: when the window is missing/expired, start a fresh 60s window; allow while count < rpm, otherwise block. Returns {allowed, win}.

nodes.name: AppConfig
nodes.summary: Per-app configuration shape: id, name, rpm limit, and monthlyTokenBudget quota.

nodes.name: GateError
nodes.summary: Thrown gate error shape {status, message}; statuses include 429 (rate limit), 402 (quota), 502 (all providers failed).

nodes.name: Usage
nodes.summary: Per-app accumulated counters: requests, tokens, costUsd, fallbacks, errors.

nodes.name: Window
nodes.summary: Rate-limit window state: {count, resetAt}.

nodes.name: Provider fallback chain
nodes.summary: Ordered provider chain (Gemini 2.0 Flash → GPT-4o-mini) with a per-call timeout; on failure the next provider is tried, and a 502 is surfaced only if all fail. ponytail: prod should only fail over on transient errors (5xx/429/timeout), not bad input.

nodes.name: Centralized provider secret
nodes.summary: Calling apps authenticate with a gateway API key only; provider keys (OpenAI/Google) live in the gateway's .env and are never exposed to callers, so key rotation/revocation is centralized.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: Narrative docs that map gateway concerns to code and give run/test instructions.

layers.name: Configuration
layers.description: Project manifest and environment template (provider secrets, port).

layers.name: HTTP entry & auth
layers.description: Thin Elysia facade: x-api-key auth, route definitions, and the endpoints it serves.

layers.name: Gateway core
layers.description: The gate: per-app registry, rate-limit + quota enforcement, provider fallback chain, cost tracking and observability.

layers.name: Policy primitives
layers.description: Pure, dependency-free policy logic extracted for isolated testing.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: Bun tests covering the rate-limit money-path.

tour.title: Overview & concern map
tour.description: Start at the README: it maps each cross-cutting concern (centralized secret, auth, rate-limit, quota, fallback, cost, observability) to the exact file/function that implements it, and lists what is intentionally omitted for prod.

tour.title: Stack & dependencies
tour.description: The package.json shows the stack: Bun runtime, Elysia web framework, and the Vercel AI SDK with OpenAI + Google providers. Note generateText (non-streaming) is chosen so usage/cost is available at the end of the call.
tour.languageLesson: Bun treats .env and TS natively; `type: module` + `bun run src/index.ts` is the whole dev loop.

tour.title: HTTP entry & auth
tour.description: index.ts is a deliberately thin facade: Elysia .derive() validates x-api-key via appByKey (401 on miss), then POST /v1/chat delegates to handle and GET /metrics returns counters. Apps never see provider keys.
tour.languageLesson: Elysia's .derive() runs before handlers and attaches typed context (`app`), so auth lives in one place instead of each route.

tour.title: The gate: limit → quota → fallback → cost
tour.description: handle() is the single function every request flows through: rate-limit (429), monthly token quota (402), then the Gemini→GPT chain with a 20s per-call timeout and fallback, recording usage/cost and emitting a JSON log line. Throw 502 if all providers fail.
tour.languageLesson: In-memory Maps hold state for the demo; ponytail: markers flag Redis (limits) + Postgres (usage/audit) for prod.

tour.title: Pure policy primitive
tour.description: rateLimit is extracted into its own dependency-free file with an injected clock, which is exactly what makes it unit-testable without timers.
tour.languageLesson: Passing `now` as a parameter (instead of calling Date.now() inside) is the difference between testable and untestable time-dependent code.

tour.title: Tests & secrets
tour.description: The test file locks the rate-limit money-path (allow-to-rpm then block, reopen on window reset). Finally, .env.example holds the provider keys server-side — the centralized-secret concept the whole gateway is built around.
